content.problemStatement:
Given a connected binary tree with **N** nodes, determine if it is height-balanced. (**Note:** A binary tree is height-balanced if the depth of the two subtrees of every node never differs by more than one.)

For example, the following binary tree is height balanced:




Input Format

The first line of the input contains a single integer NNN — the number of nodes in the binary tree.
Next N−1N - 1N−1 lines contain three space separated characters pi,ci,Rip_i, c_i, R_ipi​,ci​,Ri​, describing the tree with edge informations - pip_ipi​ is an integer denoting the parent node of the iiith edge, cic_ici​ is child node, and the letter RiR_iRi​ denotes whether the child is left child or right child, it's value is `L` if cic_ici​ is left child of pip_ipi​, else `R`.


Output Format
Output on the single line, `YES` if the tree is height balanced, else `NO`.
constraints: All pi's and ci's are distinct
content.inputFormat: The first line contains a single integer T, the number of test cases. Each test case contains an integer N, the number of nodes in the binary tree, followed by N-1 lines containing three space-separated characters pi, ci, Ri, describing the tree with edge information.
content.outputFormat: For each test case, print YES if the tree is height-balanced, else print NO.

sampleTestCases.explanation: The given binary tree is height-balanced.
The given binary tree is height-balanced.
The given binary tree is not height-balanced.

solution.approach: To determine if a binary tree is height-balanced, we can use a recursive approach to calculate the height of each subtree and check if the difference in heights is greater than 1. We start by creating a tree from the given edges and then use a recursive function to calculate the height of each subtree.
solution.timeComplexity: O(T * N) per test case, O(T * N) total, where N is the number of nodes in the tree.
solution.spaceComplexity: O(N) — the dominant structure is the tree itself.
